coredata.dc:title: One-step preparation of N-doped grapheme quantum dots with high quantum yield for bioimaging and highly sensitive electrochemical detection of isoniazid
coredata.dc:creator: Sivaselvam, S.
Viswanathan, C.
Ponpandian, N.
coredata.dc:description: Conventional techniques for synthesizing GQDs have a poor quantum yield (QY) that restricts their biological applications. Herein, we present a rapid, cost-effective and high quantum yield synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) through a scientific microwave reactor. The reaction parameters like microwave irradiation time, temperature, precursor concentration and pressure were optimized for achieving high quantum yield. The prepared N-GQDs exhibit bright blue fluorescence and excitation independent emission property with a quantum yield of 42.81%. In-vivo investigations on C. elegans revealed that the as-prepared N-GQDs are exceptionally biocompatible and maintain the normal physiological functioning of the primary and secondary targeted organs in nematodes. The synergetic effect of intestinal barrier and defecation behavior mitigates N-GQDs translocation into reproductive organs of nematode. In addition, the N-GQDs modified GCE was tested for electrochemical sensing characteristics towards the anti-tuberculosis drug isoniazid (INZ). The N-GQDs showed appreciable electrocatalytic performance towards INZ with high sensitivity (3.76 μA μM−1 cm−1). The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) analysis of N-GQDs exhibit a lower detection limit of 10.91 nM for INZ. The N-GQDs modified sensor exhibits good reproducibility, excellent anti-interference ability and excellent analytical performance for INZ in real samples like human blood serum and urine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Biocompatibility
C. elegans
Bioimaging
Electrocatalyst
Isoniazid (INZ)

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: C. elegans maintenance and the effect of N-GQDs in the primary and secondary targeted organs of nematodes
Bio-distribution of N-GQDs inside the nematode body
Physiological evaluation of N-GQDs in C. elegans
Bio-distribution and intestinal permeability of N-GQDs in C. elegans
Selectivity, repeatability, reproducibility, and stability analysis of N-GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM and XPS analysis of prepared N-GQDs. a) Low magnification, b) High magnification, c) SAED pattern and d) size distribution of as prepared N-GQDs. e) XPS survey spectrum of N-GQDs. f) High-resolution XPS C1s spectrum and g) High-resolution XPS N1s spectrum of N-GQDs.

The physicochemical characterization of N-GQDs. a) XRD diffraction pattern, b) Raman spectra, c) FTIR spectra, d) UV–visible spectra, e) photoluminescence property under different excitation wavelength and e) PL decay curve of N-GQDs at the emission wavelength of 415 nm.

Physiological effect of N-GQDs in C. elegans. a) Lifespan, b) Body length, c) Brood size, d) Head thrash, e) Body bends and f) Defecation behavior of nematodes treated with 0–200 mg/L of N-GQDs. Error bar indicates the Mean ± SEM of three independent experiments.
Translocation pattern of N-GQDs in the nematode C. elegans. Fluorescent image showing the translocation pattern of N-GQDs after exposure to 200 mg/L in nematodes.

Effect of N-GQDs on Intestinal permeability of treated nematodes. a) Fluorescent image showing the intestinal permeability of nematodes treated with 200 mg/L of N-GQDs. b) Quantification of relative fluorescent intensity. Error bar indicates the Mean ± SEM of three independent experiments. ns – non-significant.

a) CV curves for varied scan rates from 40 mVs−1 to 240 mVs−1 at N-GQDs/GCE in 0.1 M PBS solution, b) Calibration curve for scan rate studies, c) CV curves of N-GQDs/GCE at different pH medium in 0.1 M PBS solution with a scan rate of 50 mVs−1 and d) calibration plot for different pH study. Error bar indicates the Mean ± SEM of three independent experiments.
The plausible electro-oxidation mechanism of isoniazid at N-GQDs/GCE.

Differential pulse voltammetry of N-GQDs/GCE. a) DPV curve of N-GQDs/GCE with varying concentration of INZ ranging from 0.19 μM to 750 μM in 0.1 M PBS solution, b) respective DPV calibration plot with an inset of as obtained linearity values. Error bar indicates the Mean ± SEM of three independent experiments.

(a) DPV curves for interference studies done with different species in 0.1 M PBS with the addition of INZ at N-GQDs/GCE (b) Histogram plots for interference studies. Error bar indicates the Mean ± SEM of three independent experiments.

DPV curves for real sample analysis in a) Human blood serum and b) Human urine, c) Calibration plot for human blood serum analysis and d) Calibration plot for human urine sample analysis. Error bar indicates the Mean ± SEM of three independent experiments.
Schematic representation for the synthesis of N-GQDs by microwave reactor.
Effect of reaction temperature and microwave irradiation time on the Quantum yield of N-GQDs.
Analytical comparison of the N-GQDs/GCE modified electrode with previously reported INZ sensors in electrochemical detection.
INZ detection performance of N-GQDs/GCE in various real samples (biofluids) with different concentrations of INZ.

head.ce:title: One-step preparation of N-doped grapheme quantum dots with high quantum yield for bioimaging and highly sensitive electrochemical detection of isoniazid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641 046, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641 046, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conventional techniques for synthesizing GQDs have a poor quantum yield (QY) that restricts their biological applications. Herein, we present a rapid, cost-effective and high quantum yield synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) through a scientific microwave reactor. The reaction parameters like microwave irradiation time, temperature, precursor concentration and pressure were optimized for achieving high quantum yield. The prepared N-GQDs exhibit bright blue fluorescence and excitation independent emission property with a quantum yield of 42.81%. In-vivo investigations on C. elegans revealed that the as-prepared N-GQDs are exceptionally biocompatible and maintain the normal physiological functioning of the primary and secondary targeted organs in nematodes. The synergetic effect of intestinal barrier and defecation behavior mitigates N-GQDs translocation into reproductive organs of nematode. In addition, the N-GQDs modified GCE was tested for electrochemical sensing characteristics towards the anti-tuberculosis drug isoniazid (INZ). The N-GQDs showed appreciable electrocatalytic performance towards INZ with high sensitivity (3.76 μA μM−1 cm−1). The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) analysis of N-GQDs exhibit a lower detection limit of 10.91 nM for INZ. The N-GQDs modified sensor exhibits good reproducibility, excellent anti-interference ability and excellent analytical performance for INZ in real samples like human blood serum and urine samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Tuberculosis (TB) ranks as one of the most deadly and infectious diseases in the world. Medications like isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide and ethambutol hydrochloride are commonly used to prevent tuberculosis worldwide [1]. Among them, isoniazid (INZ) is the widely prescribed drug for the treatment of TB. The INZ drug is triggered by the mycobacterial enzyme catalase-peroxidase, which results in the formation of reactive oxygen species that forms an adduct with the NAD(+) and NADH(+), leading to the inhibition of lipids and nucleic acid biosynthetic enzyme [2]. The lower concentration of INZ during treatment may lead to the development of drug-resistant bacteria or ineffective treatment for tuberculosis (TB). In contrast, overdosing on INZ might cause liver damage, peripheral neuropathy, lack of appetite, and nausea as side effects [3]. The overdosing of INZ also results in environmental exposure by excretion and may lead to the development of drug-resistant bacteria, posing an alarming threat to human and environmental health. The concentration of the INZ is the key factor in encountering tuberculosis. Although the INZ has adverse effects, its effectiveness against tuberculosis makes it an unavoidable clinically administered drug. According to US Public Health Service research, an average of 18,000 patients are medicated with isoniazid each year. To prevent harmful side effects, it is crucial to monitor the patients treated with INZ periodically. Therefore it is crucial to develop a highly sensitive, cost-efficient and reliable analytical technique for accurate INZ detection in clinical diagnosis. Isoniazid levels in humans can be detected using various analytical techniques, including high-performance liquid chromatography, immunosensors, automated chemiluminescence, capillary electrophoresis, and electrochemical sensors [4–8]. Among them, electrochemical sensors have several advantages such as rapid response, ease of handling, high sensitivity, greater selectivity, and low cost, over other approaches. The sensitivity and selectivity of the electrochemical sensor depend on the nature of the electrode material and its property. Hence, the synthesis of effective electrode material is crucial for developing a highly stable and cost-effective electrochemical sensor with high sensitivity.

Graphene-based materials (GBMs) have become intriguing in the realm of electrochemical research due to their exceptional electrochemical performance. The GQDs have unique electrical and optical properties in addition to graphene's inherent characteristics, making them a versatile and promising material in numerous applications, such as energy conversion, environmental monitoring, photocatalysis, photosensitizer, CO2 reduction, bio-imaging, sensing, and so on [9–11]. GQDs can be synthesized using various methods like chemical oxidation, arc discharge, electrochemical oxidation, sonochemical, hydrothermal and solvothermal methods [12,13]. Previous reports suggested that these synthesis methods are constrained by the need for special equipment, complex procedure, time-consuming, usage of hazardous chemicals and low product yield, making them unsuitable methods for large-scale production [14–16]. In contrast, the microwave-assisted method is gaining popularity as a promising and viable strategy for synthesizing GQDs in a short period with a high quantum yield. The scientific microwave reactor offers various advantages like rapid and uniform heating within a short reaction time, easy control over the reaction parameters, low energy consumption, and high product yield when compared to other methods, making it a suitable method for large-scale production [17].

The photoluminescence property of GQDs together with ultra-small size, and photostability makes them a feasible candidate in applications such as bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery, photosensitizer, photodynamic and photothermal therapy [18–20]. Moreover, the functional groups of the GQDs can be easily modified by covalent or non-covalent chemical modification. Previous studies show that the incorporation of nitrogen into the structure of GQDs enhances electrocatalytic and optical properties [21]. Despite all of the hype around GQDs, their toxicity is still a critical factor that needs to be considered and addressed. The toxicity of GQDs should be well studied and assessed for their implementation in real-life biomedical applications. Previous studies have shown that the GQDs do not exhibit significant cytotoxicity [22]. In contrast, the in-vivo study showed contradictory results on the toxicity of GQDs by inducing oxidative stress and DNA damage [23]. Hence, determining the in-vivo toxicity, translocation of GQDs to targeted organs, and their underlying mechanisms is crucial for the practical application of GQDs in the biomedical field. A simple soil nematode, Caenorhabditis elegans was used as an in-vivo model to evaluate the toxicity and translocation of GQDs. The C. elegans is a small-sized transparent nematode with well-described genetic background and short lifespan, which makes it a suitable in-vivo non-mammalian alternative animal model to study toxicity. Our present study describes the strategy of obtaining high quantum yield N-GQDs using a scientific microwave reactor (CEM, Discover). The C. elegans in-vivo assay system was utilized to examine the biological effect on the targeted organs and translocation of N-GQDs inside the animal body. N-GQDs translocation in wild-type N2 worms has been further explained in this study. Owing to the electrical property of N-GQDs, glassy carbon electrode-modified N-GQDs were employed to assess the electrochemical sensing capability of INZ. On exposure to INZ, the fabricated sensor showed high sensitivity, good reproducibility, very low detection limit and anti-interference ability. The practicality of the prepared electrochemical sensor was evaluated by quantifying INZ in human blood and urine samples. The rapid, economical, and one-step synthesis of N-GQDs by scientific microwave reactor with high quantum yield can be further shined with dual property-seeking future perspective in biomedical research.
The chemicals and instruments techniques used in the current work are summarized in the supplementary information.

The N-GQDs were prepared by microwave-assisted pyrolysis method. Briefly, 1 g of citric acid was transferred to a clean microwave reactor vial with 1 g of urea as a nitrogen source. Then the sample was purged with N2 gas for 10 min and placed in the microwave reactor. The sample in the microwave vial was irradiated at a controlled temperature (160–250 °C) for a period of 2–10 min, power of 100 W, a pressure of 50–200 psi and ramping time of 10 min. These parameters were controlled by the software. When the temperature exceeds the melting point of citric acid, it began to melt into liquid and the color of the solution changes from translucent to dark orange. This indicates the formation of GQDs and the boiling hot orange liquid is rapidly dripped into the NaOH solution (2%) under vigorous stirring. This final sample was centrifuged at 50,000 (Beckman coulter, optima XPN) rpm and then dialyzed (3500 Da MWCO) for 2 days in water to remove unreacted compounds. The quantum yield was determined according to the previously described method [24]. The tailored parameters of N-GQDs with high quantum yield were lyophilized and used for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C. elegans maintenance and the effect of N-GQDs in the primary and secondary targeted organs of nematodes
ce:section.ce:para: The Bristol N2 (Wild-type) worm was cultured in the nematode growth media (NGM) with Escherichia coli OP50 as food and incubated at 20 °C. The age synchronized worms were used for all the experiments. The effects of N-GQDs (0–200 mg/L) on the lifespan, body length, brood size, locomotion, defecation behavior, ROS generation and intestinal permeability of nematodes are discussed in detail in the supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-distribution of N-GQDs inside the nematode body
ce:section.ce:para: The in-vivo translocation of N-GQDs in C. elegans was investigated as described previously [25]. Briefly, the age synchronized nematodes (n = 40) were treated with N-GQDs (200 mg/L) in a 24 well sterile microtitre plate. After 60 min of incubation, the worms were washed with M9 buffer and placed over a clean glass slide with a 1.5% agar pad containing sodium azide (30 mM). The incapacitated worms were analyzed under confocal microscopy and the fluorescent intensity was quantified using ImageJ software.

The fabrication of N-GQDs/GCE began with making the GCE impurity-free, which could affect the oxidation of INZ. First, the bare GCE surface was cleaned with 0.01 M of alumina slurry to a mirror clear surface and washed with DI water and ethanol. The optimized concentration of N-GQDs about 6 μL was drop cast over GCE and dried at 50 °C in a hot air oven. The as-fabricated N-GQDs/GCE was utilized for the oxidation of INZ all over the electrochemical assessment. The detailed procedure for the optimization of N-GQDs over GCE for electrochemical sensing and the real sample pretreatment are provided in the supplementary information.

The preparation of N-GQDs by microwave-assisted pyrolysis technique is a rapid, energy-efficient and economical method in comparison to other conventional methods [26]. The hydronium ion formed when citric acid is heated beyond its melting point acts as a catalyst in the breakdown process. The decomposition of urea results in the formation of isocyanic acid and ammonia. The breakdown of CA forms methyl-malic acid and byproducts like aconitic acid. The byproducts go through a complex reaction pathway and result in the formation of the aromatic cluster through aldol condensation and cycloaddition [26]. When the aromatic cluster concentration reaches the critical point, burst nucleation occurs and the N-GQDs are formed [27]. The schematic representation of N-GQDs synthesis by microwave reactor is shown in Scheme 1 .

The precursor concentration and process parameters like time, temperature, and pressure all have an impact on N-GQD quantum yield. In the present study, these parameters were optimized using a scientific microwave reactor. In the course of the optimization process, just one parameter was altered at a time, with the other parameters remaining constant. Initially, the effect of pH on the fluorescent property of N-GQDs was studied and the highest fluorescent intensity was obtained at pH -7 and it decreases at the low and high pH conditions due to the protonation and deprotonation of functional groups present on the N-GQDs surface (Fig. S1). Further, the reaction temperature was increased from 160 to 250 °C and the microwave irradiation period was raised from 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 min. Table 1 shows the quantum yield (QY) of N-GQDs synthesized under different conditions. The highest quantum yield of N-GQDs (QY: 37.5%) was observed at 180 °C and 6 min. At higher temperatures of 200 and 250 °C, the precursors undergo carbonization, resulting in lower QY [27]. To further improve the QY of N-GQDs, the concentrations of CA and urea was varied (1,1 to 3,2). The other reaction parameters were kept constant (Temperature: 180 °C; Time: 6 min; pH -7). The result showed a higher QY of 41.8% in the precursor concentration ratio of 2:1 of CA and urea (Table S1). The effect of pressure in influencing the QY of N-GQDs was studied by increasing the pressure from 10 to 200 psi. The result showed that the pressure has no significant impact on the QY of N-GQDs (Data not shown).

The N-GQDs were synthesized in a scientific microwave reactor, and their morphology was characterized by HR-TEM analysis. The HR-TEM image showed N-GQDs of uniform size (Fig. 1a and b). The SAED pattern reveals that the N-GQDs are amorphous in nature (Fig. 1c) and the average size of N-GQDs is 3 nm (Fig. 1d). The chemical purity of the N-GQDs was analyzed by XPS spectroscopy. The survey spectra of N-GQDs showed carbon, nitrogen and oxygen peaks with the atomic weight percentage of 77.5, 4.3 and 18.2% respectively (Fig. 1e). The deconvoluted high-resolution C1s spectra showed three prominent peaks centered at 284.8 eV, 285.4 eV and 286.5 eV that corresponds to CC, CN and CO groups respectively. The presence of CN peak at 285.4 eV is evident for the attachment of nitrogen with the carbon group (Fig. 1f). The XPS spectra of N1s showed two prominent peaks at 399.6 eV and 401.4 eV corresponding to the CNH2 and ONC groups respectively [26]. This result validates the successful formation of N-GQDs by the microwave method. The XRD pattern of N-GQDs showed a weak dispersed diffraction peak centered at 2θ = ~23.3°, which corresponds to disordered carbon atoms and the (002) graphite lattice (Fig. 2a). The interlayer spacing of N-GQDs was found to be 0.384 nm which is higher than that of graphite (0.33 nm).

The higher d value of N-GQDs is attributed to the addition of functional groups and nitrogen doping, which result in the increased interlayer space and our results are in agreement with previous reports [28,29]. The microstructure of the prepared N-GQDs was investigated by Raman spectroscopy and it exhibits D band and G band at 1359 and 1600 cm−1 respectively (Fig. 2b). The integrated intensity ratio of the disordered D-band and crystalline G-band (ID/IG) is a measure of disordered degree which is extensively used to characterize defects in the graphene-based material and reveals the in-plane defect of the sp2 hybridized carbon cluster.

The ID/IG value of prepared N-GQDs is 0.871, which indicates the presence of less defective carbon structure in the prepared N-GQDs [26]. The surface functionalities of N-GQDs were analyzed by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2c). The characteristic peak at 3370, 1646, 1160 and 1121 cm−1 were attributed to the OH, CC, CO and CH stretching vibrations respectively. The absorption peak at 1743 and 1420 cm−1 represents the formation of CN units in the carbon structure respectively [30]. The UV–visible spectra show two absorbance peaks at 276 and 312 nm, which corresponds to the electronic π-π⁎ transition of CC and CN respectively (Fig. 2d). The photoluminescence spectra of N-GQDs under different excitation wavelengths from 250 to 380 nm showed excitation independent emission property with a maximum emission peak at 451 nm at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. (Fig. 2e). The calculated quantum yield of the obtained N-GQDs is 41.8%. The quantum yield (QY) of GQDs prepared by different methods are compared in table S2 and the prepared N-GQDs showed higher quantum yield when compared to previous studies. The fluorescent lifetime spectra of N-GQDs showed well fitted triple exponential function with an average fluorescent lifetime of 7.22 ns, which showed promising application in biology and electronics (Fig. 2f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiological evaluation of N-GQDs in C. elegans
ce:section.ce:para: The optical property of the GQDs was employed in optoelectronics, electrochemical, environmental and biological applications [31,32]. The rapid expansion of N-GQDs in various applications raises a safety concern on the environmental organism. Biocompatibility is the major concern in biomedical applications like bio-imaging and drug delivery. The prolonged assay system of C. elegans (N2 wild-type) was used to evaluate the in-vivo effect of N-GQDs at a range of 0–200 mg/L. In the prolonged exposure system, the nematodes were exposed to N-GQDs (0–200 mg/L) from L1-L4 stage. The control group showed an average lifespan of 24 days. Exposure to N-GQDs at different concentration (0–200 mg/L) showed no significant change in the lifespan of nematodes. Further, the egg laying capacity of nematodes was studied and the result depicts no significant decline in the egg laying capacity even at 200 mg/L. The normal egg laying pattern indicates that exposure to N-GQDs has no significant effect on the secondary targeted organs of the nematodes. In the body length measurement also N-GQDs has no negative influence on the exposed nematodes (Fig. 3a-c). Further, the intestinal ROS production was examined and the treatment groups (1–200 mg/L) showed no significant influence on the induction of intestinal ROS when compared to control (Fig. S3). The induction of intestinal ROS production is generally considered as a primary response of nematodes to toxicants. Further, the locomotion behavior of nematodes was studied through head thrash and body bend frequencies. The locomotion behavior of nematodes are the important secondary targeted organs of nematodes. The results of head thrashing and body bends of treated nematodes indicate that exposure to N-GQDs (0–200 mg/L) has no adverse effect on the functioning of locomotion behavior of nematodes (Fig. 3 d&e). The defecation behavior of the nematodes is an important parameter in maintaining the normal physiological function of nematodes. The defecation behavior of nematodes are the important parameter to study the toxicity of engineered nanomaterial [33]. Worms exposed to N-GQDs (0–200 mg/L) maintains the normal defecation length of the nematodes when compared with control (Fig. 3f). These data revealed that nematodes exposed to 0–200 mg/L had no deleterious effect on the functioning of targeted organs and the prepared N-GQDs showed high biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-distribution and intestinal permeability of N-GQDs in C. elegans
ce:section.ce:para: The translocation of engineered nanomaterials into the primary and secondary targeted organs of nematodes induces toxicity formation. After prolonged exposure of N-GQDs to the nematodes (200 mg/L), confocal microscopy analysis was performed to study the bio-distribution of N-GQDs inside the nematodes body (Fig. 4 ). The result showed uniform distribution of N-GQDs in the gut and intestinal region of the treated nematodes. The absence of fluorescent signal in the secondary targeted organ depict that exposure to N-GQDs did not induce damage to intestinal barrier and maintain its normal functioning. In the secondary targeted organs of nematodes, the fluorescence of N-GQDs are not observed.

Further, a lipophilic fluorescent dye Nile red was used to determine the intestinal permeability of nematodes exposed to N-GQDs (200 mg/L). The result revealed that exposure to N-GQDs did not induce a noticeable increase in the Nile red intensity when compared to control, which indicates the normal functioning of the intestinal barrier (Fig. 5 ). The mitigation of N-GQDs into the secondary targeted organs could be due to the proper functioning of intestinal barrier and defecation cycle [25]. These results demonstrated that N-GQDs are highly biocompatible and promise a potential application in biomedical research.

The electrocatalytic performance of the as-fabricated N-GQDs was evaluated using [Fe(CN6)3−/4−] as the electrolyte medium. Initially, the fabricated electrode with N-GQDs was studied using cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique with the constant scan rate of 50 mV/s in the presence of 0.1 M [Fe(CN6)3−/4−] in 5 mM of KCl. To provide a good depiction of the electrocatalytic behavior of N-GQDs/GCE, the electrode without N-GQDs fabrication was also compared. When comparing the CV redox curves of bare GCE and N-GQDs/GCE, it is observed that the N-GQDs/GCE exhibits more intense redox peaks (Fig. S3a). The bare GCE resists the transfer of ions as there are no additional reaction materials and the higher redox current response of N-GQDs/GCE is attributed to higher electrocatalytic active sites available at N-GQDs, which rapidly transfer electrons between electrode and electrolyte ions interfacial surface. Thus, N-GQDs/GCE has higher conductivity than bare GCE. Further, we investigated the electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/GCE by altering the scan rate (20–200 mV/s). A gradual increase in the CV curve indicates the linear response at different scan rates with a shift towards positive potential at a higher scan rate. For the scan rate studies, a linear regression equation is obtained as Ipa = 3056.9x + 305.57 and R2 = 0.992 and Ipc = −3271.6x-293.85 with R2 = 0.991. The calibration plot with R2 values is included as an inset in fig. S3b. Thus, the fabricated N-GQDs/GCE surface exhibits an electrochemical response at [Fe(CN6)3−/4−] electrolytic medium. The modified and bare GCE was further analyzed to study their electrocatalytic behavior with different parameters to signify its interaction with isoniazid (INZ). Initially, the electrocatalytic behavior of bare GCE and N-GQDs/GCE were investigated in a phosphate buffer (PBS). When INZ (150 μM) was introduced, the bare GCE had no response, however, N-GQDs/GCE had excellent detection towards INZ, with an oxidation peak observed at 9.754 μA (Fig. S4a). This higher oxidation current is attributed to the anionic groups present in N-GQDs/GCE, which are actively involved in the oxidation of INZ. The unreacted bare GCE contributes to the resistance of the bare surface which does not favor the detection of INZ. A histogram of both the bare and N-GQDs modified GCE response towards INZ is depicted in fig. S4b. Further, we varied the concentration of INZ (0–210 μM) to better understand the oxidation performance of N-GQDs/GCE and observed a linear increase in the CV curve (Fig. S4c). No additional shift or overlap in the oxidation peak demonstrates the excellent sensing capacity of N-GQDs/GCE. The intriguing properties like a higher conducting substrate with plenty of electroactive sites have paved the improved response for INZ. Moreover, cationic interaction at the N-GQDs and the oxidized form of INZ lead to improved interaction at the interface. The calibration plot for different concentration of INZ showed linear value of Ipa = 0.0835x-1.0406 and R2 = 0.9954 (Fig. S4d). The active mechanism involved in the oxidation of INZ at the N-GQDs/GCE is shown in Fig. 7. Further, the scan rate (40–240 mVs−1) for the N-GQDs/GCE electrode in PBS solution was varied and obtained a linear value of Ipa = 102.13x + 5.3477 with R2 = 0.9968 (Fig. 6a and b). The pH of the electrolytic medium was further varied (pH 3–11) to investigate the optimum pH with improved enactment (Fig. 6c). Among the CV curves generated, pH -3 attained a lower oxidation current, whereas pH 5 exhibits an increase in higher current.

After reaching pH −7, the oxidation peaks started declining with the lower current for pH -9 and even lesser for pH −11. Increasing the pH value from 3 to 11, the oxidation peak current started shifting towards more negative potential (Fig. 6d). The active involvement of protons has resulted in this current and potential shift with the highest current response at pH −7. Further electrochemical studies with different parameters were done at pH −7.

Although CV studies were performed by varying different parameters, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used to detect INZ at lower concentrations and remains a sensitive technique when compared to CV [34]. Studies like different concentration variations of INZ, interference analysis, selectivity, repeatability, reproducibility, stability, and real sample analysis were done with the DPV technique. Initially, different concentrations of INZ (0.19 μM–750 μM) were consecutively added to N-GQDs/GCE at pH −7 (Fig. 8a). When the lower concentration of INZ was added, the N-GQDs/GCE was able to detect INZ with a lower current response. Slowly when the concentration was kept increasing, the oxidation peaks were also inclined to increase progressively with no overlapping of DPV curves. Moreover, a slight shift in the potential was observed at higher concentrations and it was stable after further addition. It is concluded from the DPV addition that the N-GQDs/GCE had a stable and higher detection response for INZ with efficient transfer of electrons. This higher detection response was accompanied by a higher surface area and excellent conducting nature of N-GQDs modified over GCE. The linearly obtained DPV curves were calibrated, and the attained plot is shown in Fig. 8b.

The linearity equation was Ipa = 0.2748 INZ + 1.1758 with correlation coefficient R2 = 0.993. The limit of detection (LoD) value obtained by N-GQDs/GCE for the detection of INZ was about 10.91 nM from the linear range of 0.19 μM to 750 μM. The sensitivity of N-GQDs/GCE for INZ was also calculated and acquired as 3.76 μM μA cm−1. The fabricated N-GQDs/GCE sensor had a very lower detection limit with lower sensitivity for INZ detection. Table 2 compares the N-GQDs/GCE modified electrode with previously reported INZ sensors in electrochemical detection. When compared with previous reports for the detection of Isoniazid via DPV technique, the present work showed very low LoD value of 10.91 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, repeatability, reproducibility, and stability analysis of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Several biological species and related substances have a higher possibility of interfering during the isoniazid analysis. To determine the interaction of N-GQDs/GCE with other species during INZ detection, interference studies were conducted (selectivity). While INZ detection was performed, interference species such as resorcinol (RES), glucose (GLU), dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), potassium bromide (KBr), potassium (K+), and rifamicin (RF) were all analyzed. All these interference species were added 20 folds higher concentration than INZ and injected with the addition of 150 μM of INZ (Fig. 9a). Even though, lower concentration of INZ was injected, INZ exhibited a higher oxidation current response than the other species. Thus, the selectivity of the fabricated N-GQDs/GCE sensor showed a good selectivity response for INZ and the histogram plot for the interference studies were depicted in Fig. 9b.

Repeatability tests were also performed to study the reproducibility of the N-GQDs/GCE based sensor. After several repeats done towards INZ detection, the N-GQDs/GCE displayed the same oxidation current response (Fig. S5a). Reproducibility examination was executed with five independent electrodes employing N-GQDs/GCE as a sensing platform with INZ injections (150 μM) in 0.1 M PBS solution at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1 (Fig. S5b). All the electrodes exposed for INZ detection with N-GQDs/GCE as a working electrode medium had similar and equivalent oxidation responses with a slight difference in the current response. Further, we have investigated the stability test of N-GQDs/GC electrodes for 40 days with a stability test conducted in a ten-day interval. The results were analogous to all the records done showing the astonishing sensing behavior of INZ. Fig. S5c, shows the stability histogram plot done at N-GQDs/GCE INZ detection. Thus, all the records exhibited acceptable behavior, with all the tests performed towards INZ detection with varying parameters.

An important aspect to establish is the validation of all results towards INZ detection in real-world analysis. The real sample tests were analyzed using the DPV technique with the pretreated real samples. The real samples (human blood serum and urine sample) were initially dispersed in PBS solution (0.1 M) and the INZ was added into it using the standard addition method. The DPV curves for INZ detection in real samples showed tremendous outcomes with increased oxidation current response when increasing the INZ addition.

The DPV analysis for the real sample and the corresponding calibration curve for both the real sample analysis is shown in Fig. 10. The linearity equation for human blood serum sample and urine sample are Ipa = 0.0719 INZ + 1.0737 and Ipa = 0.0703 INZ + 1.5706 with correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9988 and 0.9962 respectively. Table 3 shows the INZ detection performance of N-GQDs/GCE in various real samples (biofluids) with different concentrations of INZ. These results indicated that this biosensor is reliable for the determination of INZ in real samples.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized N-GQDs by a facile, single-step method using a scientific microwave reactor. The reaction parameters like temperature, time, precursor concentration and pressure were optimized for the high quantum yield of N-GQDs. The HR-TEM analysis revealed the narrow size distribution of N-GQDs with a diameter of about 3 nm. The N-GQDs exhibits excitation-independent emission property with a high QY of 41.8%. The in-vivo analysis in C. elegans revealed that N-GQDs have no significant adverse effect on the functioning of primary and secondary targeted organs. The proper functioning of the intestinal barrier and defecation behavior in N-GQDs treated nematodes, mitigates its translocation into the reproductive organs. Moreover, the N-GQDs showed excellent real-time in-vivo bioimaging in C. elegans. The synthesized N-GQDs exhibit excellent electrochemical sensing performance for the anti-tuberculosis drug INZ, with higher sensitivity (3.76 μM μA cm−1), broad dynamic range (0.19–750 μM), lower detection limit (10.91 nM) and good anti-interference. Furthermore, the N-GQDs based INZ sensor showed a good reproducibility rate in the determination of INZ in human blood serum and urine samples. These results showed, in-vivo bioimaging application and excellent electrochemical performance of N-GQDs which holds promising application in the field of biology and electrochemical sensing in real samples.

S. Sivaselvam: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation and writing original draft. C. Viswanathan: Formal analysis and visualization. N. Ponpandian: Resource, supervision, Project administration, Writing - Review and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank DST-FIST, DST-PURSE and UGC-SAP, Government of India for the instrumental facilities available at Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India. The authors gratefully acknowledge the Caenorhabditis Genetic Centre (CGC, University of Minnesota, MN, USA), which is funded by the NIH Office of Research Infrastructure Programs (P40 OD010440), for providing C. elegan strains. We thank Mr. Aravindan, Dr. Sanjay kanna and Dr. Kuo-Yuan Hwa from Centre for Biomedical Industry, National Taipei University of Technology, Taiwan for their help throughout the work. We also thank Dr. K. Thamaraiselvi and Dr. N. Thajuddin from Bharathidasan University for their help in analyzing Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy facility created through DST-PURSE, Govt. of India (Ref. No. SR/FT/LS-113/2009). One of the authors SS, would like to thank Bharathiar University for the University Research Fellowship (Ref. No: C2/ 7739 / 2019).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bioadv.2022.212731.
